teaching.title: Java Classes & Objects
teaching.analogy: A class is an architect's blueprint. The object is the actual building. Every building made from the same blueprint shares structure, but each has its own address, its own tenants, its own story. The constructor is the moment of construction.
teaching.content: class defines the blueprint: fields (state) + methods (behavior). new creates objects. Constructor: same name as class, no return type, runs automatically on new. this refers to the current object. Fields can have defaults.

examples.title: Class with Constructor
Multiple Constructors (Overloading)

teaching.funFact: Java's 'new' keyword allocates on the HEAP and returns a reference. The JVM's Garbage Collector automatically frees memory when no references remain — unlike C/C++ where YOU must free manually. 🗑️

teaching.title: this Keyword
teaching.analogy: this is the object's way of pointing to itself — like saying 'I' in conversation. Necessary when a parameter name shadows a field name. Also used to call another constructor from a constructor.
teaching.content: this refers to the current object instance. Used to: 1) disambiguate field from parameter (this.name = name), 2) call another constructor from a constructor (this(args)), 3) pass current object as argument.

examples.title: this Disambiguates

teaching.funFact: this() to call another constructor is called 'constructor chaining'. It MUST be the very first statement in the calling constructor. This avoids code duplication when constructors share initialization logic! 🔗

mcq.q: What is a constructor in Java?
options: A special method same name as class, no return type, runs on object creation
A method that returns the class
mcq.explanation: Constructor: Same name as class, no return type (not even void). Runs automatically when 'new ClassName()' is called. Initializes the object's state.

mcq.q: What does 'this' refer to inside an instance method?
options: The current object (the specific instance the method is called on)
mcq.explanation: this: The current INSTANCE. When neo.attack() is called, inside attack(), this == neo. Used to access fields when parameter names conflict.

mcq.q: Player p = new Player('Neo', 100). What does 'new' do?
options: Allocates memory on heap, calls constructor, returns reference
mcq.explanation: new: Allocates heap memory for the object, calls the constructor to initialize it, and returns a reference to the new object.

mcq.q: Can a class have multiple constructors?
options: Yes — constructor overloading with different param lists
mcq.explanation: Yes — Constructor Overloading: Same rules as method overloading. Different parameter lists → different constructors. Common: Item() and Item(String name) and Item(String name, int value).

mcq.q: What is the purpose of this.name = name in a constructor?
options: Assigns constructor parameter 'name' to the field 'this.name' (disambiguates)
mcq.explanation: Disambiguation: When field and parameter share the same name, this.name refers to the FIELD, plain 'name' is the parameter. Without this, name=name just assigns param to itself!

coding.title: Constructor syntax
coding.objective: Class Dog has field name. Constructor to set it:
coding.hint: Constructor has same name as the class — Dog.

coding.title: this disambiguation
coding.objective: Field 'score'. Param 'score'. How to set field in constructor?
coding.hint: this.score = the field. Plain score = the parameter.

coding.title: Create object
coding.objective: Dog class with constructor Dog(String name). Create Dog named 'Rex'.
coding.hint: new keyword creates a new object on the heap.

options: Constructors have NO return type — not even void. Remove 'void': public Car(String model)
model needs to be field first
debugging.answer: Constructors have NO return type — not even void. Remove 'void': public Car(String model)
debugging.hint: Adding void makes it a regular method named 'Car', not a constructor. Constructors have no return type.

teaching.title: Access Modifiers & Encapsulation
teaching.analogy: Encapsulation is the principle of 'need to know'. A bank account hides its internal balance variable (private) and only lets you interact through deposit() and withdraw() methods. No direct access to the safe — only through the authorized channels.
teaching.content: Access modifiers: private (class only), package-private (default, same package), protected (class + subclasses), public (anywhere). Encapsulation: make fields private, expose controlled access via public getters/setters.

examples.title: Encapsulated Class

teaching.funFact: Java IDEs (IntelliJ, Eclipse) can auto-generate getters/setters with one keyboard shortcut. But in modern Java (Java 14+ records), getters are generated automatically: record Point(int x, int y) {} — gives you x() and y() for free! 🎁

mcq.q: private field — who can access it?
options: Only the class it's declared in
mcq.explanation: private: Most restrictive. Only code within THE SAME CLASS can access private members. Not even subclasses or same-package classes.

mcq.q: Why make fields private and use getters/setters?
options: Controlled access — getters/setters can validate, log, or compute values before expose/change
mcq.explanation: Controlled Access: With private + setter, you can validate (if amount>0), log changes, compute derived values. Direct public field access has no validation layer.

options: public: accessible from anywhere; private: only within the same class
mcq.explanation: Scope: public = accessible from ANY class anywhere. private = only within the declaring class. protected = class + subclasses + same package.

mcq.q: Naming convention for a getter for field 'score'?
mcq.explanation: getX() convention: Java follows JavaBeans convention — getFieldName() for getters, setFieldName(value) for setters, isFieldName() for boolean getters. IDEs and frameworks depend on this!

coding.hint: private limits access to the declaring class only.

coding.objective: Setter that only sets age if > 0:
coding.hint: Only set if age is positive (greater than 0).

options: hp is private — can't access directly outside Player. Use a setter: p.setHp(100);
debugging.answer: hp is private — can't access directly outside Player. Use a setter: p.setHp(100);
debugging.hint: private fields are invisible outside the class. Expose via public setter method.

prediction.explanation: set(-5): -5>0 false → ignored. set(42): 42>0 true → val=42. get()=42.

teaching.title: static Fields, Methods & final
teaching.analogy: A static field is shared by ALL objects of a class — like a scoreboard on the wall that every player can see and update. Instance fields belong to each player's personal sheet. static = shared; instance = personal.
teaching.content: static field: shared across all instances (class-level). static method: belongs to class, no 'this', can't access instance members. final field: constant, must be set in constructor or at declaration. static final = constant (convention: UPPER_CASE).

examples.title: static and final

teaching.funFact: Java's Math class is all static: Math.sqrt(), Math.abs(), Math.PI. You never create a Math object — you call Math.method() directly. The Math class even has a private constructor to prevent instantiation! 📐

mcq.q: static field vs instance field — difference?
options: static: one copy shared by all objects; instance: each object has its own copy
mcq.explanation: Shared vs Personal: static field has ONE copy for the class — all instances read/write the same variable. Instance field: each object has its own copy.

options: No — static methods have no 'this' (they belong to the class, not an instance)
mcq.explanation: No this: static methods aren't associated with any instance. 'this' has no meaning — there's no current object. They can only access other static members.

mcq.q: public static final int MAX = 100 — what is this?
options: A constant (compile-time constant, cannot be changed)
mcq.explanation: Constant: static final = a class-level constant. By convention, constants are UPPER_CASE. The value cannot be changed after initialization.

coding.title: Static field
coding.objective: static int that counts all Player instances:
coding.hint: static field is shared across all instances — one 'count' for all Player objects.

options: static method can't access instance field 'rpm' — no 'this'. Make rpm static or pass it as parameter.
debugging.answer: static method can't access instance field 'rpm' — no 'this'. Make rpm static or pass it as parameter.
debugging.hint: static methods don't have 'this'. They can't access instance (non-static) fields.

prediction.explanation: n is static — shared. Each new A() increments n. Three objects created: n=3.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE OOP ARCHITECT
teaching.analogy: The OOP Architect designed all of Java's class system. It knows every field, every constructor, every access modifier rule. Prove your object-oriented foundations are unshakeable!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 6 Java: constructors (no return type), this disambiguation, constructor chaining, private/public/protected, getters/setters, encapsulation, static fields (shared), final constants, static methods (no this).

examples.title: Full OOP Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ Constructor: same name, NO return type
✅ this.field = param  (disambiguation)
✅ this(args) = constructor chaining
✅ private field + public getter/setter
✅ Validation logic in setters
✅ static field: ONE copy for ALL objects
✅ static method: NO this keyword
✅ static final: UPPER_CASE constant
✅ new = heap allocation + constructor

teaching.funFact: Java's record keyword (Java 16+) auto-generates constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), and toString() from field declarations. record Point(int x, int y) {} creates a fully immutable class in one line! 🪄

mcq.explanation: No Return Type: Constructors have NO return type specified — not even void. Adding any return type makes it a regular method, not a constructor.

mcq.q: class Item{private int val; int get(){return val;}} From outside: Item i=new Item(); i.val?
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: private makes val inaccessible outside the class. Must use i.get() to access the value.

mcq.q: static int count=0 in Player. 3 Player objects created. count=?
mcq.explanation: Shared: count is ONE variable for ALL Player instances. Each constructor call (new Player()) typically increments it. After 3 objects: count=3.

mcq.q: In a static method, this.field causes:
options: Compile error — static methods have no 'this'
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: static methods aren't associated with any object. 'this' doesn't exist. Cannot access instance fields/methods.

mcq.q: Best practice: BankAccount balance field should be:
mcq.explanation: private + methods: Encapsulation principle. Private balance prevents direct modification. Public methods (deposit/withdraw) enforce validation rules.

coding.title: Boss: Complete class
coding.objective: class Coin{private int value; getter returns value as int?}
coding.hint: Returns the int field 'value' → return type is int.

coding.title: Boss: Static count
coding.objective: static int instances=0; Each new obj increments. After 4 new calls: value?
coding.starterCode:
// 4 new MyClass() calls, each does instances++
// instances = ___

options: 'void' makes it a regular method, not a constructor. Remove 'void': public Person(String name)
debugging.answer: 'void' makes it a regular method, not a constructor. Remove 'void': public Person(String name)
debugging.hint: Constructors have NO return type. 'void Person()' is just a method named Person, not a constructor!

prediction.explanation: n is instance field — each object has its own. a.inc() twice: a.n=2. b.inc() once: b.n=1.
